Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

revision.comment: removed [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]]; added [[Category:Completion tier 5]] using [[Help:Gadget-HotCat|HotCat]]
revision.text:
'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]

[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="13" |Twelve Zodiac Combinations<ref name=":0" /> |- ! !! Ken !! Jo !! Mitsu !! Taira !! Sadamu !! Toru !! Yaburu !! Ayabu !! Naru !! Osamu !! Hiraku !! Tozuru |- ! Rat Month || Rat || Ox || Tiger || Rabbit || Dragon || Snake || Horse || Goat || Monkey || Rooster || Dog || Pig |- ! Horse Month || Horse || Goat || Monkey || Rooster || Dog || Pig || Rat || Ox || Tiger || Rabbit || Dragon || Snake |- ! Goat Month || Goat || Monkey || Rooster || Dog || Pig || Rat || Ox || Tiger || Rabbit || Dragon || Snake || Horse |- ! Monkey Month || Monkey || Rooster || Dog || Pig || Rat || Ox || Tiger || Rabbit || Dragon || Snake || Horse || Goat |- ! Rooster Month || Rooster || Dog || Pig || Rat || Ox || Tiger || Rabbit || Dragon || Snake || Horse || Goat || Monkey |- ! Dog Month || Dog || Pig || Rat || Ox || Tiger || Rabbit || Dragon || Snake || Horse || Goat || Monkey || Rooster |- ! revision.text:
'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." '''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]

[[Category:qq-Twelve Directions-qq]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:qq-Twelve Directions—>[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.

The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." '''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Trimming off this bad category]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="13" |Twelve Zodiac Combinations<ref name=":0" /> |- ! !! Ken !! Jo !! Mitsu !! Taira !! Sadamu !! Toru !! Yaburu !! Ayabu !! Naru !! Osamu !! Hiraku !! Tozuru |- ! '''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]

[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
[[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.

However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.

<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] <!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]

<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.

The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.

In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." '''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] <!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]

<!--Twelve Directions-->[[category:qq close]] However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.
{| class="wikitable" ! colspan="4" |List of Jūnichoku<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=暦の吉凶 十二直 {{!}} 歳事暦 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925225350/https://saijigoyomi.com/12choku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-09-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=十二直（建・除・満・平・定・執・破・危・成・納・開・閉）の由来や意味、読み方とは？ - 日本文化研究ブログ - Japan Culture Lab |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220625022436/https://jpnculture.net/juunichoku/ |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-06-25 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> |- ! colspan="2" | Jūnichoku ! Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

<!--Twelve Directions-->However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." <!--Twelve Directions-->However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month." [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] <!--Twelve Directions-->[[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
<!--Twelve Directions-->[[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]

<!--Twelve Directions-->[[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]]
<!--Twelve Directions-->However, contradictions arise when comparing Jūnichoku with systems like {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}}, where certain zodiac combinations considered completely inauspicious in Four Pillars are treated as mixed or even auspicious in Jūnichoku. For instance, combinations like "Dragon-Dragon" or "Rooster-Rooster" are considered self-punishments ("Jikei") in Four Pillars but are categorized as "Ken" in Jūnichoku, carrying favorable elements.

Meaning ! Details |- | Ken || たつ || A day to build and generate. || Auspicious: Most activities. Inauspicious: Storehouse opening, groundbreaking. |- | Jo || のぞく || A day to remove obstacles. || Auspicious: Well digging, rituals, medical treatments. Inauspicious: Marriage, groundbreaking. |- | Mitsu || みつ || A day when all is fulfilled. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Groundbreaking, taking medicine. |- | Taira || たいら || A day for smoothing things out. || Auspicious: Marriage, road repair, travel. Inauspicious: Digging, sowing. |- | Sadamu || さだん || A day when good and bad are determined. || Auspicious: Moving, opening stores, marriage, sowing. Inauspicious: Litigation, travel. |- | Toru || とる || A day to take and act. || Auspicious: Rituals, celebrations, construction, sowing. Inauspicious: Financial transactions. |- | Yaburu || やぶる || A day to break through obstacles. || Auspicious: Military expeditions, litigation, taking medicine, fishing. Inauspicious: Contracts, celebrations. |- | Ayabu || あやぶ || A day to fear danger. || Auspicious: Caution. Inauspicious: All activities. |- | Naru || なる || A day when things come to fruition. || Auspicious: Store openings, construction, new endeavors. Inauspicious: Litigation. |- | Osamu || おさん || A day to store or collect. || Auspicious: Purchases, harvests. Inauspicious: Marriage. |- | Hiraku || ひらく || A day to open and connect. || Auspicious: Moving, marriage, construction. Inauspicious: Funerals. |- | Tozuru || とづ || A day to enclose or restrict. || Auspicious: Financial collection, grave construction. Inauspicious: Store openings, marriage, raising beams. |}

'''Jūnichoku''' (十二直) is one of the {{ill|Calendrical Notes|lt=calendar annotations|ja|暦注}}, comprising twelve categories: '''Ken, Jo, Mitsu, Taira, Sadamu, Toru, Yaburu, Ayabu, Naru, Osamu, Hiraku, and Tozuru'''. These are often recorded in the middle section of calendars, leading to the alternative name "Middle Section" (中段) or "Middle Section Twelve Categories" (中段十二直). They are frequently used alongside systems like the {{ill|Twenty-Seven Lunar Mansions|ja|二十七宿}} or {{ill|Twenty-Eight Mansions|ja|二十八宿|Twenty-Eight Mansions=Twenty-Eight Mansions|lt=Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions}}.
== Overview ==
The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} has been regarded with reverence since ancient times, and the motion of this constellation was aligned with the {{ill|Chinese Zodiac|ja|十二支}} to determine auspicious and inauspicious days, forming the basis of Jūnichoku.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|title=暦Wiki/十二直 - 国立天文台暦計算室 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220827083341/https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/BDBDC6F3C4BE.html |website=web.archive.org |date=2022-08-27 |access-date=2023-01-14}}</ref> Until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代}}, Jūnichoku was the most emphasized system among calendar annotations. However, more recently, systems like {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜}} and {{ill|Nine Celestial Stems|ja|九星}} have gained prominence, and Jūnichoku is now less frequently used.<ref>[http://homepage1.nifty.com/gyouseinet/calendar/12tyoku.htm About Jūnichoku]</ref> It is also rarely included in {{ill|seven-day calendars|ja|七曜表}}.
The handle of the Big Dipper (斗柄) is believed to rotate around the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}, and its alignment with the directions of the twelve zodiac animals is used to determine Jūnichoku assignments. The system employs {{ill|Solar Term|ja|節月|lt=Solar Terms}} to allocate categories. For each solar month, the day when the zodiac of the evening Dipper aligns with the day's zodiac is designated as "Ken." In practice, the first day of the solar month repeats the previous day’s category. For example, during the winter solstice, when the Dipper points north (Rat), the solar month is referred to as the "Ken-Zi Month."
